critic_raw:
The design describes an RNN with attention mechanisms for longitudinal patient symptom timelines, yet employs a stratified random split explicitly justified by 'the assumption that each patient's timeline is independent after accounting for observed variables.' This is a category error. The RNN's entire value proposition is modeling temporal dependencies within a patient's sequence. If temporal autocorrelation is weak enough to permit random splitting, then the RNN has no structural advantage over the logistic regression baseline — the hypothesis is self-undermining. Either the temporal structure matters (in which case random splitting invalidates the evaluation), or it does not (in which case the RNN cannot plausibly outperform a static model on that basis).

With 1,500 patients and a complex RNN with attention, overfitting is a severe risk. An RNN processing 3 years of timestamped symptom logs with attention will have far more parameters than justified by 1,500 observations even with sequence-level batching. The design mentions no explicit regularization strategy, dropout rates, or early stopping criteria — elements that are non-optional at this sample size.

The primary outcome variable is 'quality of life score improvement at 6 and 12 months,' yet the design uses MAE as the primary metric without specifying whether these are modeled as two separate targets or a single composite. If the 12-month outcome is used as a label, any feature derived from events after 6 months constitutes future leakage relative to the 6-month prediction horizon. The design does not separate these prediction horizons or state how they are jointly evaluated.

The baseline is logistic regression on 'demographic and static clinical features,' but this baseline does not receive the same temporal feature representation as the RNN. A fair comparison requires a baseline that at least uses hand-engineered temporal aggregates (slope of symptom severity, count of treatment changes). The current setup disadvantages the baseline by design by denying it temporal information entirely.

MAE is applied to 'quality of life score improvement' without specifying the scale or distribution of that score. If scores are bounded (e.g., 0–100 point scale), an MAE of 5 is very different from an MAE of 5 on a raw PROMIS score. Clinical meaningfulness of reported MAE cannot be assessed without this anchoring.
all_issues_raised: The stratified random split assumption of 'weak temporal autocorrelation' directly undermines the hypothesis: if temporal dependencies are too weak to require temporal splitting, the RNN has no structural advantage over logistic regression, making the comparison uninformative.

With only 1,500 patients, the RNN with attention is severely underconstrained — no regularization strategy, dropout rates, or early stopping criteria are specified, making overfitting the dominant failure mode at this sample size.

Predicting 'quality of life improvement at 6 and 12 months' as a composite metric without separating prediction horizons risks future leakage if any features are derived from events between the 6- and 12-month timepoints.

The logistic regression baseline is deliberately denied temporal features while the RNN receives them, making the comparison structurally unfair — a fair baseline would use hand-engineered temporal aggregates from the same symptom timeline data.
MAE is reported without anchoring to the clinical scale of the outcome variable, making the practical significance of any reported improvement uninterpretable.
all_issues_adjudicated: The stratified random split assumption of 'weak temporal autocorrelation' directly undermines the hypothesis: if temporal dependencies are too weak to require temporal splitting, the RNN has no structural advantage over logistic regression, making the comparison uninformative.

With only 1,500 patients, the RNN with attention is severely underconstrained — no regularization strategy, dropout rates, or early stopping criteria are specified, making overfitting the dominant failure mode at this sample size.

Predicting 'quality of life improvement at 6 and 12 months' as a composite metric without separating prediction horizons risks future leakage if any features are derived from events between the 6- and 12-month timepoints.

The logistic regression baseline is deliberately denied temporal features while the RNN receives them, making the comparison structurally unfair — a fair baseline would use hand-engineered temporal aggregates from the same symptom timeline data.
